Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: RIGHT moves player right and slightly down. Player at rows 20-23, cols 48-50. Target A at rows 24-27, cols 44-47. DOWN only works at right edge.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to right edge, then DOWN to target A's row, then LEFT to align.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN didn't work this time! The player stayed at rows 20-23, cols 60-62. Earlier, DOWN worked when the player was at rows 20-23, cols 60-62 and moved to rows 24-26, cols 60-63. But now it didn't work.
Wait, let me check. Earlier:
- Player at rows 20-23, cols 60-62
- DOWN → rows 24-26, cols 60-63
- DOWN → rows 24-26, cols 60-63 (no change)
So DOWN worked once from rows 20-23 to rows 24-26, but the second DOWN didn't change anything.
Now, the player is at rows 20-23, cols 60-62 again, and DOWN didn't work. Maybe DOWN only works once per position, or there's some other condition.
Let me try RIGHT again to see if it moves the player. If RIGHT moves the player to a new position, then maybe DOWN will work from there.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The player seems to move along a path. Maybe the path is a loop, and the player can only move in one direction along the path. The directions (UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT) control which segment of the path the player moves along.
Let me try to map out the path by trying different actions and seeing where the player goes.
Actually, let me try LEFT from the current position to see if it moves the player toward target A.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: DOWN didn't work at right edge this time (player stayed at rows 20-23, cols 60-62). Earlier DOWN worked once from same position. Movement seems path-dependent with state.
Plan: Try LEFT to move player toward target A.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT from right edge
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT from right edge')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player after LEFT: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Try LEFT again
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT again')
action(['LEFT'])

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player after 2nd LEFT: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Check target A
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Target A: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Player after LEFT: rows [24-27], cols [57-59]
Player after 2nd LEFT: rows [24-27], cols [57-59]
Target A: rows [24-27], cols [44-47]
